data.title: Tim Davie: BBC culture will never be fixed
data.description:
The outgoing BBC director-general has said that staff culture at the broadcaster will never be “fully fixed” as he answered questions from employees about the Scott Mills controversy.

Tim Davie said it became “very clear” that the Radio 2 presenter had to be sacked when the corporation received new information about a historic allegation of sex offences.

Mills is the latest in a string of high-profile names to leave the BBC in disgrace, including Huw Edwards and Gregg Wallace. The late Jimmy Savile was also found to have committed multiple offences while employed by the BBC.

On his last day in the job, Mr Davie held an all-staff call where he faced questions about Mills’s sacking and about the broadcaster’s culture more widely.

Asked whether staff culture had changed during his five-year tenure, he said: “It’ll never be fully, fully fixed, but I think it’s changing, I really do.

“I think if you come in and behave in a way that some of this industry saw 20 years ago, it just would not be acceptable, you want to create an environment where it’s just ludicrous to do that.”

The BBC was aware in 2017 that Mills was the subject of a police investigation into allegations of sexual offences. The investigation did not result in charges because prosecutors decided there was insufficient evidence to proceed.

It decided to act after learning recently that the alleged victim was under 16 at the time, the BBC reported.

‘Trying to act fairly’

Sally Nugent, the BBC Breakfast presenter, asked Mr Davie when the corporation had realised that there was a problem with Mills.

He responded: “We’re trying to act fairly. It was new information quite recently that we received that made it very clear about the decision we had to make.” He said the sacking had come as “a real shock to the organisation”.

Mr Davie said that there were people in senior positions in the industry “who have had a lot of power, and if they misuse it, that has not been called out, let’s face it”.

“I do think we’ve reached a point where people behaving badly now, you can see we’re not going to tolerate it,” he said.

Mr Davie and Deborah Turness, the head of news, announced in November last year that they would be standing down after The Telegraph exposed widespread bias at the BBC, including a Panorama documentary that misled viewers by doctoring a speech by Donald Trump. The US president is suing the BBC for billions of dollars.

Rhodri Talfan Davies, an executive director on the BBC board, will spend six weeks as interim director-general before Mr Davie’s permanent replacement, Matt Brittin, a former Google executive, takes over on May 18.

Asked what he was proudest of, Mr Davie said it was “all the amazing creative work” produced by the BBC.

“The heartbeat of this operation is journalistic and editorial. I love the work, I love the fact this year we will be celebrating 100 years of [Sir] David Attenborough – I’ve met a few [great people] in my job, but you meet David Attenborough and you go ‘OK this is it, this is the beating heart’,” he said.

data.title: London finance giant in £3.5bn court row for ‘wiping out’ shareholders
data.description:
Hundreds of employees are suing a London finance giant over claims its private equity owners wiped out their shareholdings.

A $4.6bn (£3.5bn) legal battle against City giant FNZ and 17 of its current and former directors is scheduled to go ahead in May as part of a bitter row raging within the company.

Hundreds of former and current employees, who own a 23pc stake in FNZ, claim the value of their shares has been heavily diluted by a series of fundraising deals.

The employee shareholders say the transactions effectively rendered their shares in the company worthless while institutional investors were able to increase their stakes on preferential terms.

This includes Mike Stevens, a former FNZ employee who claims his shares lost 80pc of their value, jeopardising his retirement plan and forcing him to carry on working. In total, he fears he has lost around £8m.

“We worked and sacrificed a lot for FNZ and were told we would be rewarded for it,” he said. “To see something that you’ve worked so hard for go to someone else is just heartbreaking.”

Significant losses

FNZ makes online investment software used by some of Britain’s biggest banks and financial institutions, including Santander, Barclays and Aviva. Despite being founded in New Zealand in 2003, its main operations are run from London.

The software it sells to the world’s top banks is used by 26 million people worldwide to manage £2tn worth of savings, including 12 million investors in Britain.

At the heart of the dispute are three share sales that took place between May 2024 and April 2025.

It saw the firm sell newly issued shares to a small cohort of private equity firms and large institutional investors, as it pursued an aggressive growth strategy that had triggered significant losses.

The claimants argue that the deals unfairly benefited institutional investors while punishing employee shareholders.

The institutional investors include Motive Partners, a New York private equity firm that first invested in FNZ four years ago.

The case has also highlighted claims of a coup at FNZ after its original leadership were replaced with new directors.

That includes Blythe Masters, who was one of Motive’s founding partners before she was named FNZ’s chief executive in 2024.

Singapore’s sovereign wealth fund, Temasek and Generation Investment Management, a private equity firm chaired by Al Gore, the former US vice president, are also investors in FNZ.

The employees’ class-action lawsuit will now be heard in a New Zealand court in May this year after the investors failed to halt the case.

A spokesman for FNZ said: “FNZ has always considered the claim to be entirely without merit and looks forward to presenting its position formally.

“We are confident that our directors have at all times acted in the best interests of the company, its clients, its employees and its shareholders.

“The investments by institutional shareholders reflect a strong commitment to the company’s long-term growth and success, an outcome that can only be in the best interests of all FNZ’s stakeholders.”

Motive Partners, Generation Investment Management and Temasek declined to comment.

data.title: We could start fracking in the UK within months, says developer
data.description:
Gas could be fracked from Britain within months if Labour overturns a ban on the practice, a leading energy company has said.

Francis Egan, the chief executive of Australian-owned Cuadrilla Resources, said it could take as little as three months to get a gas well drilled. Production could start shortly thereafter.

The Iran war has revived the shale gas industry’s hopes of creating an “inland North Sea” in Lancashire and the East Midlands, as Britain scrambles to counter a looming energy crunch.

Cuadrilla began fracking at Preston New Road, Lancashire, in 2017. But the project was eventually scuppered when Rishi Sunak, the then prime minister, banned the practice in 2022.

“This is the second wake-up call after the Ukraine invasion, which was not that long ago. I don’t know how many more of these the politicians need,” said Mr Egan.

Cuadrilla has plugged its wells and is maintaining the site, but Mr Egan said he was ready to go back in.

“We’re keeping the lights on at Preston New Road. We’re trying to hang on to the licences,” he said.

“You could drill a well within three months in Preston New Road. It’s not a big task to get a rig, go out in the field and drill a well.”

‘The size of the prize’

Cuadrilla is not alone. Europa Oil & Gas is hoping that North Yorkshire County Council will approve next month an application to drill an appraisal well just north of Scarborough.

Planning officers have recommended that councillors approve the project, but local activists are fighting the proposal.

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, praised community activists last year who had “fought back” against shale gas exploration. He promised to pass a law that would “ban fracking and send the frackers packing”.

But Mr Egan said that “nothing is forever in politics”. If the Iran war had put Britain’s North Sea oil and gas reserves back up for debate, fracking could be too.

“Shale is not like drilling a well in the North Sea – it’s like the whole North Sea. That’s the size of the prize,” he said.

Cuadrilla’s operation repeatedly breached tough limits on seismic tremors, prompting frequent mandatory shutdowns. The company also faced court challenges from anti-fracking campaigners, and on-again-off-again moratoriums from successive Conservative prime ministers.

Nigel Farage, the Reform UK leader, has supported a resumption of fracking, but there is no sign of any Labour about-turn.

Mr Egan said: “The Prime Minister seems to have outsourced everything, energy-wise, to Ed Miliband. And Ed Miliband has pretty much outsourced it to Extinction Rebellion. And it’s more extinction than rebellion, I’m afraid.”

Cuadrilla’s owner, Australian-listed mining services company AJ Lucas, has written off the value of its UK investment on its balance sheet and reduced the company to a skeleton operation.

“But they have still valued the option. And of course, given the events of the last two or three weeks, that option value has probably increased,” Mr Egan said.

Some energy analysts are sceptical that Britain’s Bowland Shale, which stretches from Lancashire across to Yorkshire and the East Midlands, is as viable for fracking as the Permian Basin in the US.

Matt Drinkwater, from analysis firm Energy Aspects, said: “Because the UK is so much more densely populated than the US, fracking is much higher cost. There’s a lot of expenditure required for mitigation of impacts on the local community, and also for the social licence to operate.”

While shale gas might be viable when gas prices are high in the coming few years, by 2028 it might be uncompetitive again and the industry would not even be up and running by then.

Mr Egan acknowledged that there was still technical work to be done to ensure that fracking the Bowland Shale would produce the right gas flows over the right time frame.

However, he said developing a domestic shale industry would give Britain lower-cost gas and an economic boost. “We’re talking tens of thousands of jobs and billions of tax revenue.”

data.title: British AI startup faces backlash over Microsoft data centre
data.description:
One of Britain’s largest artificial intelligence (AI) startups is facing a backlash over plans to build a data centre for Microsoft after local officials raised fears it would drive up energy costs for residents.

Nscale, which counts Sir Nick Clegg as a board member, has been hit with objections in Essex over concerns the power-hungry energy demands of the site will strain the local electricity grid.

The planned data centre, built on a former scaffolding warehouse in Loughton, has been billed as “the UK’s largest AI supercomputer” and will be backed by 50 megawatts of AI processors. 

One of its key customers will be Microsoft after the two companies signed a deal to develop AI power. 

However, Loughton Town council officers have now demanded fresh evidence from Nscale to prove the development’s “substantial energy demand” will not “strain local grid capacity or increase costs for residents”.

The objection was sent to officials at Epping Forest District Council who are overseeing the application. It notes that the site is 50pc higher than originally proposed and has 50pc more internal capacity.

“The proposal would require more cooling and increased energy to facilitate this application,” the objection warns.

The town planning officer has requested that Nscale submit a full new planning application to proceed with the plans. The timeline to get the project up and running has also been pushed back from this year to early 2027.

It is understood that the delay is to allow time to secure access to the most up-to-date AI processors from US chip giant Nvidia, which were only announced this month.

Nscale has been cited by the Government as a key driver of Britain’s AI infrastructure revolution.

Earlier this month, the company, led by Australian entrepreneur Josh Payne and headquartered in the UK, confirmed it had raised $2bn to expand across the US.

The business has secured investment from backers including Nvidia, Dell and Norwegian investor Aker. Its site in Loughton is expected to house thousands of Nvidia graphics processors and will count Microsoft as a key customer.

AI systems such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT and Anthropic’s Claude require large amounts of computing power and have fuelled hundreds of billions in investment around the world in data centre capacity.

Labour has been courting AI data centre developers as it seeks to engineer an AI infrastructure building spree in Britain and has sought to ease planning constraints.

Last week, The Telegraph disclosed that Steve Reed, the secretary for housing, had bypassed objections from councillors in Buckinghamshire to give ministers the final say over a £2bn data project.

Meanwhile, the global building blitz of AI data centres has prompted concerns over their ballooning energy demands and water use, clashing with Labour’s stated net zero agenda.

Last month, a report from the energy regulator Ofgem admitted that the total pipeline of UK data centre projects included power demands of more than 50 gigawatts (GW).

If all these projects were built and operated at full capacity, they would require more power than Britain’s peak daily electricity.

An Nscale spokesman said: “Site investigation and permit work is under way on the Loughton site, and we expect construction work to begin in the second quarter of 2026.

“While the schedule was recently updated to accommodate the installation of the latest Vera Rubin 200 technology, we expect the site to be operational in the second quarter of 2027.”

data.title: Ofcom under fire for handing £50,000 to The Guardian’s charity
data.description:
Broadcasting watchdog Ofcom has come under fire after handing £50,000 to The Guardian’s charity.

Filings show Ofcom provided £50,000 in funding to The Guardian Foundation last year to support its Untold Stories project, which teaches news and media literacy skills to children in impoverished areas of Greater Manchester and London.

In addition to outreach programmes, the scheme invites young participants to a dedicated classroom in The Guardian’s London headquarters to learn about news reporting.

Ofcom regulates TV and radio, rather than the newspaper industry. However, its decision to channel funding to a charity run by a news outlet has sparked controversy.

Nigel Farage, leader of Reform UK and a presenter on GB News, said: “This is exactly the sort of cosy media club that GB News seeks to disrupt. Payments like this continue to raise questions about Ofcom’s role in the media landscape.”

Ofcom said the Untold Stories scheme was part of its Making Sense of Media programme, which forms part of its statutory duties to support media literacy. The two-year contract was won by The Guardian Foundation following a tender process last year.

But Lord Young of Acton, the Tory peer and founder of the Free Speech Union, said the payment was particularly concerning given The Guardian is not a member of the press regulatory Ipso, instead policing complaints internally.

He said: “It’s genuinely odd that Ofcom has given £50,000 to The Guardian when, almost alone among UK newspapers, The Guardian eschews any regulation of its content.

“If you’re misrepresented by The Guardian, your only recourse is to complain to its internal ombudsman – in other words, it marks its own homework. If only Ofcom granted the same latitude to GB News and Elon Musk.”

Ofcom’s battles

Ofcom’s work has been thrust increasingly into the spotlight in recent years, especially as its new role overseeing online safety laws has put it up against US tech giants and triggered accusations of censorship.

Meanwhile, the launch of opinionated broadcaster GB News has sparked rows over free speech.

The watchdog last year lost a high-profile High Court battle against GB News over its ruling that the channel had breached rules about the use of politicians as presenters in programmes fronted by Sir Jacob Rees-Mogg.

It marked the first time Ofcom lost a legal challenge against one of its decisions on broadcasting standards. The regulator’s advisory fees jumped to £4.6m last year after it forked out for lawyers, while it was also ordered to cover GB News’s costs.

An Ofcom spokesman said: “As part of our statutory duties to improve media literacy, we commission a wide range of community and grassroots organisations through our Making Sense of Media programme. Untold Stories is one of these projects.

“The Guardian Foundation – which is separate to The Guardian’s news operation – was awarded the contract to deliver the work through a robust and competitive tender process.”

A spokesman for The Guardian Foundation said: “The Guardian Foundation’s Untold Stories project delivers resources and workshops in schools and at our education centre.

“The aim is to support young people to develop their voice, think critically and build resilience against misinformation. Led by our education experts, the project will provide vital data to inform future work across the sector.”

The Guardian Foundation was set up to promote press freedom and access to liberal journalism around the world. Its primary funder is the Scott Trust, the £1.2bn fund that owns The Guardian.

Alongside media literacy initiatives, it also provides support to independent media organisations and administers the Scott Trust Bursary scheme, which provides tuition fees for student journalists as well as paid placements and the opportunity to work at The Guardian after graduation.

data.title: Labour has no plan for the worst energy shock of our lives
data.description:
Keep calm and carry on – or something to that effect.

It’s week five of Donald Trump’s Iran excursion, and just when you think the president couldn’t sound any more deranged, he’s now talking about taking Kharg Island by force. 

This is an idea military strategists have compared with anything from a “suicide mission” to a “self-imposed hostage crisis”.

Either that, or Trump could decide to blow the offshore terminal to smithereens instead; such is the clear and rational thinking that pervades the White House these days.

The price of crude oil responded with another 3pc jump on Monday, taking it to $116 (£87.94) at one point – a roughly 50pc increase since hostilities began. Expect it to go much higher if the US puts boots on the ground in Iran.

Meanwhile, there is talk that repairs to damaged energy infrastructure could take between two and five years, prolonging the disruption to global supplies and keeping prices ruinously high.

Indeed, many experts already believe the Middle East conflict is shaping up to be the worst energy shock the world has ever faced.

As Fatih Birol, the head of the International Energy Agency, has warned, the Iran war has knocked out roughly 11 million barrels of oil a day compared with the loss of five million barrels during each of the two crises in 1973 and 1979.

Gas shortages have been similarly devastating, with about 140 billion cubic metres a day lost – double what was removed from the market after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.

So what, might you ask, is our Government’s plan to address what could prove to be an even bigger blow to our livelihoods than both the pandemic and Vladimir Putin’s imperialist expansion on Europe’s doorstep?

The answer, it seems, is that there isn’t one. On the contrary, ministers are still – publicly, at least – persisting with the absurd stance that it is pretty much “situation normal”.

Yes, contingency plans are being drawn up. “You would expect any government to plan for eventualities where events happen in the world,” Bridget Phillipson, the Education Secretary, said breezily at the weekend.

However, “people should be reassured, ahead of the Easter holidays, just to carry on as things are”, she added, seemingly oblivious to the irony of how anxiety-inducing such words are.

While ministers attempt their desperate “nothing to see here” act for fear of “scaring people”, as Phillipson patronisingly puts it, those same people can turn on the news and see that two things are happening.

The first is the supply squeeze becoming more acute worldwide. Bangladesh may have only a few weeks of oil left, swathes of Southeast Asia have only a few months’ worth of supplies at best, parts of Africa are experiencing shortages, and so too are Australia and New Zealand.

True, many of the countries facing a critical supply squeeze are more reliant on Middle Eastern oil, while we, on the other hand, get most of our crude from the US and Norway. 

However, much more of our refined fuels are imported, which is why the price of diesel has gone up 26pc versus 15pc for unleaded petrol, so it’s only a matter of time before Europe is next.

Indeed, industry figures think the crunch is just around the corner. Wael Sawan, Shell’s chief executive, has warned of energy and fuel shortages by mid-April. That’s a fortnight away. 

Traders and fuel suppliers estimate that diesel stockpiles could halve by mid-May.

I know who I’m inclined to listen to, and it’s not the Education Secretary, nor any Cabinet member, for that matter. The Government is being far too complacent.

The second thing that people in this country can see is that while ministers dilly-dally, a growing number of countries are acting quickly and decisively, starting with sweeping tax cuts to soften the hit to household incomes.

Spain’s socialist government was among the first to move, cutting VAT on fuel, electricity and gas by more than half to 10pc as part of a €5bn (£3.8bn) emergency support package. 

Ireland cut fuel duty by a fifth, while the Polish government slashed VAT on fuels from 23pc to 8pc, and reduced excise taxes to the lowest level permitted by Brussels.

At the weekend, Anthony Albanese, the Australian prime minister, joined in by halving fuel duty – a move expected to save motorists 26 cents a litre when they fill up their tanks – as well as unveiling a new four-stage fuel security plan to manage supply.

Other than support for those reliant on heating oil, there has been no such relief here.

Steps to bring down both energy and fuel consumption are also being rapidly introduced, though again not in the UK. 

India has shut down factories, Australia has introduced free public transport to discourage people from driving, civil servants in the Philippines are working a four-day week, and Slovenia has become the first European country to introduce rationing.

None of these represents a silver bullet, but surely any action is better than none at all? Why not start here by designating filling stations for emergency vehicles and prioritising diesel for commercial vehicles?

It is ludicrous for our Government to pretend we are somehow immune to the pressures being felt everywhere else, and it is even sillier to think we can’t handle a few small sacrifices for the greater good of the country. 

Reducing unnecessary journeys, turning off the lights, turning down the heating and going on fewer holidays would be nothing compared to the restrictions everyone endured during Covid.

Our thoroughly forgettable Prime Minister would much rather have more pointless meetings than take tough decisions – the latest with representatives from the shipping, insurance and energy industries to discuss the “importance of working closely with the private sector in tackling the crisis” – whatever that means.

For Rachel Reeves, it has reached the humiliating point where bosses are avoiding her altogether. 

First, the petrol retailers gave the Chancellor the cold shoulder because of accusations of “profiteering”, then several supermarket bosses were too busy to discuss rising food prices with her. Devoid of ideas, this Government has been reduced to populist show trials.

At the very least, it can scrap the planned fuel duty rise, yet Phillipson insists there is “no need” to. It is a farcical position to take, and it sets the scene for yet another screeching Labour policy reversal in the coming weeks. 

No wonder business bosses have stopped listening. Then again, hasn’t everyone?

data.title: 120-year-old British wheel factory at risk of closure
data.description:
A 120-year-old British wheel factory is at risk of closure after its German owners called in the administrators.

Moveero, which makes wheels, rims and hubs for off-road vehicles used in farming and construction, said its UK arm had become insolvent on Thursday, putting around 250 jobs at risk.

Administrators from Interpath have been appointed to run the struggling business, which trades from a historic factory in Telford, Shropshire.

Its customers include JCB and Caterpillar, among others, with its products used for the wheels of everything from earthmovers to airport tugs and tree harvesters.

The business, dating back to 1908, was part of GKN’s automotive division but was sold to German private equity firm Aurelius in 2020.

Aurelius was also behind a disastrous buyout of ethical beauty chain the Body Shop, which collapsed just five months after its new owners took control.

In a statement on Thursday, Ryan Grant, Chris Pole and Sam Birchall, administrators at Interpath, said Moveero had been battling “significant operational challenges”, including weak demand for its products and tough competition.

Rising interest rates have made buying heavy equipment more difficult for farmers and construction companies, along with trade tariffs imposed on imports by the likes of the US and China.

Moveero Ltd reported a loss of £4.4m in 2024, the latest year for which there are accounts, after sales fell 26pc to £32.6m.

The wider Moveero group, which includes businesses in the US and Denmark, continues to operate profitably and is not insolvent.

In the UK, administrators have been appointed to “protect the interests of creditors”.

A statement said this would “allow the UK businesses to continue trading while buyers for the businesses and their assets are pursued, with all staff retained during this period”.

David Geraghty, Moveero’s chief executive, said the company’s customers had also provided emergency funding that had “given us the additional time we need to continue to explore the options available”.

Mr Grant, the managing director at Interpath, added: “We have been pleased with the positive conversations and financial support provided by customers in recent days, which has ultimately provided the businesses with a crucial lifeline, enabling us to trade while we explore options for the business.”

Interpath said its “immediate priority is to seek buyers for the UK businesses and assets” and urged potential buyers to come forward as soon as possible.

Aurelius has been approached for comment.

data.title: London’s biggest taxpayer in line for windfall
data.description:
London’s biggest taxpayer is in line for a major payday after his trading firm posted record profits.

Alex Gerko’s XTX raked in £1.7bn in profits in 2025, up 33pc from the previous year after cashing in on market volatility. Revenues also rose 44pc over the year from £2.7bn to £3.9bn.

Details of his latest pay were not included in XTX’s latest results, which were published on Companies House overnight.

However, last year’s results led to Mr Gerko, who owns 75pc of the business, paying himself £682m. The remaining profits were split between the company’s 30 partners.

The prospect of a significant payday could cement the Russian-born mathematician’s status as London’s highest taxpayer.

He paid £331.4m in personal tax last year, according to The Sunday Times, making him the second-highest taxpayer in the UK behind Betfred’s Fred and Peter Done.

Mr Gerko, who is a British citizen, has previously said he would be supportive of a wealth tax.

However, he has been locked in a dispute with HMRC in recent years over claims he was hit with “massive double taxation”.

XTX uses cutting-edge machine learning technology at a supercomputer in Iceland to predict activity across a range of markets, including stocks, bonds, currencies, commodities and crypto.

It handles more than $250bn (£189bn) in transactions on a daily basis, using sophisticated algorithms to secure a modest but lucrative edge when trading.

Mr Gerko, 46, started his own trading firm in 2015, having moved to London from Moscow to become a currency trader at Deutsche Bank.

He renounced his Russian citizenship in 2022 after Vladimir Putin’s invasion of Ukraine.

His firm has since grown exponentially to become one of Britain’s most profitable companies, making its founder one of the richest people in the world.

The so-called “king of the geeks” has committed more than £350m to charities and good causes since 2020, establishing XTX as one of the UK’s largest donors.

This includes a string of maths schools after he became dismayed at the state of British education on the subject, as well as conservation efforts and the London Symphony Orchestra.

He lives in north London with his wife and three children and also owns the 150-acre Holme Wood estate in the Chiltern Hills, where he was previously embroiled in a local planning dispute over attempts to build a 110-metre pond.

Mr Gerko himself has a PhD in maths and focuses his philanthropy on giving young people in Britain the same opportunities to study maths as he had in Russia.

On Thursday, XTX pledged $40m to a charity that funds scholarships for students who win medals in prestigious scientific competitions, including the International Mathematical Olympiad.

data.title: Majority of Labour voters back drilling in the North Sea
data.description:
A majority of Labour voters support drilling for oil in the North Sea, a new poll has found.

In a blow to Ed Miliband, a survey has revealed that supporters of drilling outnumber the opponents by more than two to one amongst his party’s voters.

The Energy Secretary has repeatedly dismissed suggestions that Britain should ramp up domestic oil and gas production, even as the crisis in the Middle East has piled pressure on global supplies.

But a poll by campaign group Looking for Growth suggests Labour’s supporters – and a majority of voters in the country – disagree with him.

The poll found that support for drilling for oil in the North Sea stood at about 57pc amongst all voters, compared to only 15pc who were against.

Within this, support amongst Labour voters stood at 51pc while 21pc were opposed. Another 22pc said they neither agreed nor disagreed, with 5pc saying they didn’t know.

Even amongst backers of the eco-focused Green Party, supporters for North Sea drilling narrowly outnumbered opponents by 38pc to 33pc.

Support amongst Conservative and Reform voters topped 70pc.

Some 49pc of Labour voters also indicated they supported onshore fracking of oil and gas – although just 40pc were happy for it to take place in their area.

Lawrence Newport, the chief executive of Looking for Growth, said: “There has been no government plan to reduce bills, have abundant energy and stable long-term power.

“Instead, politicians have adopted policies that have left the lives of millions across the country at the mercy of international events.

“When successive governments give in to the consultants, lawyers and lobbyists, the energy bills of the British people pay the price.

“This new polling shows that voters of all parties want a broken system fixed and to be prioritised again.”

Looking for Growth’s poll also asked voters about nuclear power and found that 61pc supported it, compared to 14pc who opposed it.

The debate over North Sea drilling has intensified in recent weeks following the US-Iran conflict, which sent energy prices soaring.

The RAC said on Tuesday the crisis had pushed the cost of filling up a typical family car with diesel beyond £100 for the first time since 2022.

The cost of filling up a petrol car has similarly increased by around £11, bringing the total to £85.

Most supporters of increasing domestic oil and gas production say it is unlikely to directly lower costs, as both commodities are priced on international markets, but that it could improve energy security, generate higher tax revenues and protect jobs.

Mr Miliband has introduced a ban on new drilling in the North Sea and has so far resisted pressure to rethink his position.

Speaking to Labour MPs this month, he reportedly said: “Gas is bought and sold on the international market, so whether it comes from the North Sea or is imported, it is charged at the same price.”

He went on to praise the amount of money raised by a renewable energy auction earlier this year, saying technology such as wind turbines and solar panels was “homegrown power that we can rely on”.

The Conservatives accused the Energy Secretary of dogma and called on the Government to cut taxes on domestic energy production.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said earlier this week: “It is unforgivable that Labour are continuing with Ed Miliband’s mad plan to shut down our own energy supplies in the North Sea in the middle of an energy crisis.

“Keir Starmer is too weak to stand up to Ed Miliband, who has allowed radical activists to make us weaker, poorer and more dependent on foreign imports with higher emissions through endless lawfare.”

A Government spokesman said: “Regardless of where it comes from, oil and gas is sold on international markets, which set the price for British billpayers – making us a price taker.

“The only way to truly protect ourselves from these price spikes is to get off the rollercoaster of fossil fuel markets.”

data.title: Honda and Sony scrap £70,000 electric car in last-minute about-turn
data.description:
Honda and Sony have abruptly cancelled plans for a £70,000 electric vehicle (EV) amid a slowdown in demand.

The two Japanese giants said on Wednesday they would scrap the launch of Afeela, an electric sedan that had been scheduled to go on sale later this year.

The duo had established a joint venture, known as Sony Honda Mobility, four years ago in an effort to tap into rising demand for battery-powered cars. The Afeela was scheduled to be built at a plant in Ohio, with production trials starting last year.

However, the cancellation is a surprise because the carmakers had been so close to launching the vehicle.

Afeela showrooms had been open for customer demonstrations and sales were expected to begin in the US in the coming months.

At the Consumer Electronics Show in Las Vegas, just weeks ago, they also revealed plans for a new SUV and claimed its new vehicles would “evolve” modern cars from “driver-centric machines into intelligent partners”.

‘Challenging earnings situation’

Honda disclosed a $15.7bn (£11.7bn) write-down on its EV division this month, blaming a slowdown in demand for battery-powered vehicles in the US.

Honda said it had invested heavily in electric cars as “striving for carbon neutrality is a responsibility Honda, as a manufacturer of mobility products, must fulfil for the future”.

But it admitted its cars business division had “fallen into an extremely challenging earnings situation”.

The Japanese business said Donald Trump’s tariffs had hit the profitability of its vehicle sales in America, while a decision by the president to scrap EV incentives had dampened consumer demand.

It also warned of rising competition from cheap Chinese rivals that offer greater value to consumers.

It announced it had cancelled three of its own EVs that had been planned for the US market, and this week confirmed the end of its Afeela project with Sony.

Sony Honda Mobility said on Wednesday that Honda’s “reassessment of its automobile electrification strategy” had “fundamentally altered” plans to develop and launch the Afeela.

It said the venture’s owners had agreed to launch a review of the plans for Sony Honda Mobility.

Honda, Japan’s second-biggest carmaker, has repeatedly lowered its ambitions for EVs as it battles waning demand and competition from China, switching instead to launching new hybrid models to appeal to more drivers.

It also cut its EV sales target last May from 30pc of vehicles to 20pc. Toshihiro Mibe, Honda’s chief executive, said at the time it would also reduce investment in electrification by about three trillion Japanese yen (£14bn) by 2031.

data.title: The Scott Mills fiasco is the latest proof the BBC has no idea how to manage talent
data.description:
The latest BBC talent debacle, this time involving Scott Mills, may look to some like a one-off piece of awkward editorial housekeeping. But it is increasingly difficult to see these moments as isolated incidents when they recur with such wearying regularity. Step back from the immediate noise and a broader truth comes into focus: the BBC does not primarily have a talent problem, it has a talent management problem – and it has done for years.

The list of names is now long enough to make that argument impossible to dismiss. Those who have exited amid storms of controversy include Jeremy Clarkson, Jonathan Ross, Danny Baker, Gary Lineker and Jermaine Jenas.

Then there is the vile category all of its own, occupied by Jimmy Savile, Stuart Hall and Rolf Harris, alongside Huw Edwards. These are cases of a morally different order and should never be casually mixed in with matters of contract handling, editorial judgment or career stewardship.

But set all those appalling examples to one side, and there still remains a substantial roster of presenters and performers whose treatment by the BBC has ranged from clumsy to short-sighted to downright wasteful.

Think of Tony Blackburn, Miriam O’Reilly, Paul Gambaccini and, to a lesser extent, Ken Bruce, Simon Mayo and Steve Wright, all figures whose relationships with the corporation were poorly handled or prey to the sort of institutional weather that blows through Broadcasting House whenever somebody feels the urge to rearrange the furniture.

Too often, moving talent around appears to be treated as a way of demonstrating dynamism. It allows a newly installed executive to look decisive, and it costs little in budgetary terms. What it can cost, however, is trust, loyalty, audience connection and the goodwill of the very people upon whom the BBC’s public identity depends.

That is because the BBC has never fully come to terms with what it actually is. For all its complexity, it remains at heart a talent organisation. It is built on the shoulders of individuals whose voices, faces, judgments, rhythms and quirks become familiar to millions.

Audiences do not form relationships with management structures, strategy documents or internal memos. They form relationships with people. And those people are not standard employees. They are performers, communicators, broadcasters, interpreters of mood, tone and public feeling.

They are often mavericks, sometimes difficult, usually sensitive, frequently edgy, and almost always operating under levels of scrutiny that most of the members of the organisation around them would find intolerable if it were applied to their own daily work.

That difference matters. You can tell whether on-air talent has had a good day at work because you can hear it or see it for yourself. Their failures are public, their triumphs are public, and the judgment upon them is immediate. The teams who support them are often excellent, but they are not exposed to that same relentless test. It follows, surely, that people doing this kind of work require a different kind of management. Not mollycoddling, not a free pass for bad behaviour, but a more intelligent, more structured and more informed kind of stewardship.

That has long been my view, and it is not a theory formed from the cheap seats. My first BBC Network contract came as Barry Norman’s reporter, and from there I went on to work alongside a range of remarkable BBC talent, including Anne Gregg, Anne Robinson, Jeremy Vine, Angela Rippon (and, indeed, Huw Edwards).

I spent years as on-air talent myself, albeit without quite the star wattage of some of those names, and I came to understand at close quarters the peculiar demands of the job: the scrutiny, the pressure, the emotional wear and tear, the often blurred line between the public role and the private self, and the way in which talent can flourish brilliantly in the right environment or fray in the wrong one.

Alongside that, I also spent 25 years running my own agency, Excellent Talent, representing and curating talent. The likes of Harry Enfield, Bob Harris, Johnnie Walker, Helen Lederer, Christopher Timothy, Brian Perkins and James Cosmo were all stars on our books. Looking after talent is not the same as managing any other category of staff. The skill lies in recognising that difference without becoming overprotective or indulgent, and in building structures that support both creativity and accountability.

In 2016 I wrote to then director-general, Tony Hall, suggesting that the BBC should create an internal talent agency within itself, to nurture and understand talent, and deal with the mercurial people that often occupy these roles.

What would an internal BBC talent agency have done in practice with some of the examples I mention above? It would not have waved a wand and abolished scandal, stupidity or wrongdoing. No serious person could claim that. But it could have created a form of specialist stewardship; it might have made a substantial difference to the outcomes, or at the very least to the amount of collateral damage.

Take Paul Gambaccini. After he was arrested over an unfounded abuse claim in 2013, he endured an extraordinary period of uncertainty while his reputation was shredded by suspicion before any charge was ever brought. He has said that during that time the BBC made him feel like a “non person”.

An internal talent agency would not, of course, have interfered with a police investigation, but it could have ensured that from the moment he was caught up in that nightmare, he had a dedicated internal advocate who understood both the legal sensitivity and the devastating reputational exposure involved in being a recognisable BBC voice under that kind of cloud.

It could have co-ordinated legal liaison, communications advice, mental health support, internal decision-making and, crucially, protected the presumption of innocence from being trampled inside the building by fear and optics. In Gambaccini’s case, the BBC looked more like an institution trying to distance itself from risk than one trying to handle a valued broadcaster with fairness.

The same principle applies, although differently, to Miriam O’Reilly, dropped from Countryfile in 2009, in an act of age discrimination (she successfully sued, with this argument). There, the issue was not criminal suspicion but the mishandling of a visible presenter.

It is a good example of where a proper talent agency could have made a material difference. That dispute did not simply erupt overnight. It reflected a failure of internal communication, expectation setting and the handling of perceived career transition.

Someone specialising in talent management would have seen that as exactly the sort of situation requiring early intervention: honest conversations about editorial direction, changes in role, age sensitivity, future opportunities and how those discussions were framed.

Too often in large institutions, those matters are handled clumsily by layers of management who neither understand the performer’s sense of professional identity nor appreciate how quickly bad handling can turn into grievance, humiliation and litigation. An internal agency would have been there to broker, challenge and reality-test those conversations before they hardened into a public dispute.

Ken Bruce is perhaps the most obvious example of pure mishandling rather than scandal; he was asked to leave for his new job at Greatest Hits Radio 17 days earlier than first planned. Here was one of the most successful and trusted voices in British radio, yet his departure was managed in a way that felt oddly graceless and needlessly bruising.

A proper internal talent agency would have understood that someone like Bruce was not simply a presenter occupying a slot in a schedule, but a long-term asset whose exit needed to be managed with care.

That means honest conversations early, sensible planning around succession, dignity in departure and an understanding that how you treat one star is watched carefully by every other star in the building. Instead, the handling created precisely the kind of noise and bad feeling that better stewardship should avoid. Even when talent is leaving, management matters.

The BBC has spent years behaving as though talent were both essential and faintly embarrassing, celebrated when delivering ratings and attention but regarded with suspicion the moment that same prominence creates complexity. That is not management. That is institutional ambivalence dressed up as professionalism. And it is one reason why the corporation keeps finding itself in the same muddle, dealing with the fallout of mishandled relationships, inconsistent standards, reactive decision making and a chronic failure to distinguish between strong management and bureaucratic box-ticking.

When I wrote back in 2016, I suggested I would be happy to run the internal talent agency – but received no reply. I would make that case again now to the incoming director-general Matt Brittin with even greater conviction, because the evidence has only mounted in the years since.

Because until the BBC starts managing talent as though it truly understands the peculiar, exposed, high-wire nature of the work, it will go on repeating the same mistakes, wasting the same gifts and learning the same lessons far too late. And for an organisation built so heavily on the relationship between audience and performer, that is not a minor operational weakness. It is a failure to understand the very thing that made the BBC powerful in the first place.

Jon Briggs is a former BBC Radio 5 and Radio 2 presenter, original UK voice of Siri and The Weakest Link. The first episode of his new podcast, The Price of Fame, features Ken Bruce.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: British factories brace for an energy crisis worse than the 1970s
data.description:
Europe’s fuel supplies could be squeezed within days, and governments need to act.

That is the ominous warning from Wael Sawan, the boss of Shell boss, who this week said “a ripple effect” could see the continent’s energy security hit “as we get into April”.

A growing number of governments across the world are already introducing exceptional measures to limit the amount of fuel people use as the world reels from the Middle East energy supply shock triggered by war in Iran.

South Korea’s residents have been told to drive less, take shorter showers and use washing machines and vacuum cleaners only on the weekend.

In Sri Lanka, the government has declared that public institutions should take Wednesday off. The island nation has also implemented fuel rationing, with drivers now limited to buying 15 litres of petrol or diesel a day for private cars.

Thailand, with its tropical climate, has called on workers to swap suits for t-shirts in an effort to reduce the use of air conditioning. Swimming pools are no longer being heated in Mauritius and fountains have been turned off at the government’s direction.

All that may seem like a distant concern – something affecting far-off lands with vastly different economies and climates to ours.

Yet restrictions are already beginning to hit Europe. Slovenia became the first EU country this week to introduce fuel rationing, limiting drivers to 50 litres a day.

Here in the UK, ministers insist fuel supplies are secure and are urging people to go about their days as normal. Yet if this crisis drags on, that may not last.

The Telegraph has reported that strict speed limits could be imposed under the National Emergency Plan for Fuel, should ministers choose to activate it.

Adam Bell, a former government energy official at consultancy Stonehaven, said home-working guidance may also be likely if the Government decides to bring in measures to curb energy consumption in the coming months.

“If you are going to do anything in the short term, it will be that,” he says.

“If you do not have enough fuel, governments have to think very carefully about how they use the fuel they have, what is still coming in, and what they hold in inventories,” says Callum MacPherson, the head of commodities at Investec.

Ashley Kelty, of investment bank Panmure Liberum, said limits on the amount of gas factories can use may also be considered.

Even if the Government doesn’t step in, manufacturers may adopt self-imposed limits as costs skyrocket.

The average annual electricity bill for small industrial and commercial businesses has risen by 20pc, or £96,000, to £578,000 since the war in Iran began, figures from Cornwall Insight show.

For those businesses that use gas – including chemical producers, glass and ceramics factories and metalworks – the pain is even worse. Average annual gas bills have risen by nearly 60pc to just more than £1m.

“Many of these companies are already battling slimmer margins, so this rise in energy costs is not something they can simply absorb,” Jacob Briggs, from Cornwall Insight, says.

“For some firms, this could mean the difference between investing in growth this year or shelving their plans entirely, and for others, high bills could force some very difficult economic decisions.”

Arjan Geveke, the director of the Energy Intensive Users Group, told The Telegraph last week there was a “high risk” that factories would start voluntarily cutting production in the coming weeks because of high energy costs.

Kelty fears that the impact of the crisis on the UK economy could be “horrendous”, with the automotive and steel manufacturing sectors the worst affected.

He said: “Even if the rockets stop tomorrow, the problem is that it’s going to take a month to get back to any form of normality in terms of restarting oil production and getting all the tankers out of the Gulf and to their original destinations.”

The UK will face a “very challenging” winter, as even if the conflict comes to an end, it will struggle to refill its gas storage, Kelty added.

All this is set to weigh on the economy. If the energy crisis continues and Brent crude oil remains at around $100 a barrel, Capital Economics has forecast that the UK will enter a mild recession by the end of the year.

Jennifer McKeown, chief global economist from Capital Economics, said: “The energy-intensive sectors of industry, hotels and retail are likely to be hit the hardest.

“The sharp increase in the cost of energy is going to mean that it is quite likely that industrial production will need to be cut back.”

In the summer of 2022, as energy prices soared following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, many European nations imposed policies to curb oil and gas consumption.

Measures at the time included restrictions on air conditioning and reduced lighting for national monuments and advertising boards.

By late 2022, Britons were encouraged to cut energy use during peak times, with some electricity providers offering reduced costs in an effort to encourage households to shift consumption to off-peak hours.

Restrictions were far more draconian during the Arab oil crisis of the 1970s. In 1973, drivers were using fuel ration books and were advised to reduce their maximum speed to 50mph.

By early 1974, fuel shortages and rationing meant that many industrial businesses were operating with a three-day working week and TV channels were shut off after 10.30pm.

Fatih Birol, executive director of the International Energy Agency (IEA), has said that the current energy supply shock is worse than the 1970s. “No country will be immune to the effects of this crisis if it continues to go in this direction,” he has warned. “So there is a need for global efforts.”

The IEA has called for car-free Sundays globally and urged people to work from home three days a week in order to conserve fuel.

Ministers in Britain are understood to have found this intervention unhelpful. With the Government convinced the UK has enough fuel for the moment, their biggest concern is to avoid panic buying or unnecessary changes in business patterns that could create fresh problems.

However, disruption to energy supplies looks likely to continue. QatarEnergy, which operates the world’s largest liquefied natural gas facility, said on Tuesday that it was preparing to declare “force ‌majeure” on some fuel contracts.

The crisis measure would allow it to stop shipments agreed under contract without facing any legal liability. Italy, Belgium, South Korea ⁠and China will be affected.

MacPherson said: “If this went on and on, and we had to live in a world where there is much less oil than we have been used to in the past, people would have to travel less, and when they do travel, it would be more expensive.”

While Britain may have escaped the worst of it so far, the threat of an energy-triggered economic crunch looms.

data.title: Housebuilder facing cash squeeze handed £300m by government
data.description:
Labour is under pressure to explain why the Government has handed a troubled housebuilder more than £300m in taxpayer-backed loans and contracts.

Paul Holmes, the shadow housing minister, said there were “questions about Labour’s judgement” after it partnered with Vistry to deliver affordable housing.

The London-listed building firm has been handed around £315m in grants and loans, according to Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister.

Homes England, the housing quango responsible for allocating the funds, does not directly monitor the financial viability of the companies it works with, he added.

Shares in Vistry have crashed by more than 50pc over the past month following the surprise departure of Greg Fitzgerald, chief executive, and amid concerns about a cash squeeze at the business.

Vistry has announced plans to offer deep discounts on some of its homes to offload them and raise capital. Reports suggest it has also demanded delays on paying subcontractors and asked for discounts as it battles to preserve cash.

It follows a string of profit warnings after bosses underestimated the cost of building homes in the south of England.

Paul Holmes, the Conservative shadow Housing minister, submitted an official parliamentary question to Mr Pennycook last week on the taxpayer’s “financial commitment to Vistry”.

“Vistry’s plight raises serious doubts about Labour’s ability to deliver their so-called Affordable Homes Programme,” Mr Holmes told The Telegraph.

“And it also raises questions about Labour’s judgement. If the Government isn’t properly scrutinising the companies it partners with, how can it say whether they will succeed or fail?

“Labour must come clean about how and why ministers concluded Vistry was the best choice – and tell us how much taxpayers’ money could be lost.”

In November, ministers cleared Vistry to bid for as much as £700m in taxpayer subsidies as part of the Government’s £39bn affordable homes programme. It has the same status under the existing programme, begun by the Conservatives in 2021.

Anthony Codling of RBC Capital Markets said there were “amber flags” about Vistry’s financial position.

“Vistry needs to slow down, shrink the business, stabilise the cash situation before it sees growth,” he said.

Mr Codling added that the going concern note in Vistry’s latest financial accounts had grown from around 350 words to more than 1,700 words, three times the size that it was this time last year, which “paints a darker downside” than before.

The wider economic backdrop also offers little comfort, with housebuilders warning of inflationary uncertainty stemming from the conflict in Iran.

“If the market really turns down, it’ll get a lot trickier for Vistry,” said Aynsley Lammin of Investec.

Mr Pennycook said that the Affordable Homes Programme does not carry hard credit risk exposure on companies it works with, as it is a grant programme, so a “purposely high-level review” is undertaken instead.

The ministers added that there are “ no checks undertaken on the off-balance sheet liabilities” as it “is not the role of Homes England to undertake liquidity tests on strategic partners”.

A Vistry spokesman said that taxpayer money was not at risk and said it “has been deployed on building affordable homes that this country desperately needs”.

They added that Vistry “continues to maintain a comfortable level of headroom against its banking facilities”.

“We work closely with Homes England on the delivery of new homes, and, like all of its partners, our programme is closely monitored and audited,” they said.

“The grant funding has all been committed, and the homes for which the funds were allocated are now contracted and owned by regulated, registered providers.”

In March, Mr Fitzgerald vowed to shrink Vistry’s debt by the end of this year.

A spokesman for Homes England said: “Homes England operates within robust, transparent frameworks when working with delivery partners. We don’t comment on the commercial or financial position of individual partners.”

The Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Heathrow’s new car parks to cost more than Gatwick’s second runway
data.description:
From HS2 to Crossrail, British infrastructure projects lead the world in one unenviable respect: their sizeable, often runaway budgets.

The bill for big-ticket construction schemes typically starts off with eye-watering sums – and then climb to even more staggering levels as budgets are blown and costs run rampant.

Now a plan to provide 36,000 parking spaces at Heathrow airport is poised to join the pantheon of cash-hungry mega-projects after it was revealed that it will come with a staggering £2.6bn price tag.

That’s £400m more than the estimated outlay for a new runway at Gatwick and more than half the cost of Heathrow’s Terminal 5 – the largest free-standing structure in the UK.

At £72,000 per parking space, the two multi-storey car parks planned under the scheme will also be comfortably the most expensive in Britain and a contender for the costliest ever built.

Heathrow argues the price per space will be lower because only half the overall cost, about £1.3bn, is for the buildings themselves, with the rest of the spending going towards associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Yet the airport has faced criticism from some of the world’s largest airlines, who will ultimately pick up the bill for the world’s most expensive car park.

In a week when National Car Parks (NCP), the country’s biggest car park operator, collapsed into administration, the plan might seem an ambitious one.

However, unlike NCP, which was hit by a drop in commuting, Heathrow can rely on a captive customer base and funding rules that mean construction costs are ultimately likely to be passed on as aircraft landing fees.

Airline bosses have attacked the project as typical of Heathrow’s largesse in lavishing cash on gold-plated enhancements that they and their customers must ultimately pay for.

The airport says the car parks will be a vital component of its £49bn plan for a third runway that will lift passenger numbers from 84 million a year to as many as 150 million.

It says the sites – one next to the M4 and the other to the south near the M25 – will be needed to cope with the thousands of additional passengers driving to what is already Europe’s busiest airport each day, even if a higher proportion of people take public transport.

And it argues that the expense of the project reflects construction costs in London that are themselves amongst the highest in the world.

But Luis Gallego, the boss of British Airways (BA) owner International Airlines Group, said the plan was overpriced.

He highlighted the shortcomings of a funding model that allows Heathrow to charge airlines a regulated fee to cover building and operating costs.

“We support expansion, but not at any cost. All future spending must be carefully controlled as Heathrow already charges passengers twice as much as Amsterdam and three times as much as Paris,” he said.

Willie Walsh, the director general of the International Air Transport Association, was blunter, describing the car parks plan as “madness” and saying his mind “boggled” at the £2.6bn bill.

He said Heathrow had proposed what would have been the world’s most expensive car park when he was chief executive of BA as part of a previous runway plan that was rejected in 2010.

He said: “You can find data on what it costs to build a car park, there’s lots of evidence there, and Heathrow is just way out of line with the rest of the world.

“The problem is that they are incentivised to spend as much money as possible and to build the most expensive car park and to be proud of that.”

Heathrow has said that calling the new structures car parks does not do them justice.

Thomas Woldbye, the airport’s chief executive, has instead referred to them as “parking villages”.

The blueprint for the new runway proposes “two large parkway car parks” that would replace more than 20 existing facilities, with the northern site able to accommodate 20,000 vehicles and the southern one 16,000.

It adds: “This could support fast and efficient connections to each terminal campus via dedicated transport systems.

“These new parkways could also provide local communities with improved access to airport terminals and public transport links and help reduce congestion on local roads.”

While a leaked Heathrow briefing paper states the cost of the parkways to be £2.6bn, a spokesman said the parking areas alone will consume only about half the budget, with the rest covering the cost of associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Major profit centre

The car parks may be necessary to cope with an influx of new passengers, but they would also be a major profit centre, with parking fees contributing £200m in revenue last year.

Heathrow’s long-stay car parks currently charge £46.80 for the first day and £37.40 for each additional 24-hour period.

Taking an average of £40 per day, the number of spaces planned could generate more than £500m in revenue over the course of a year.

That is not including income from drop-off charges and the short-stay car parks nearer to terminals that the airport has indicated it plans to retain.

Michael O’Leary, the chief executive of Ryanair, which does not fly from Heathrow, said drop-off fees were “a scandal” across UK airports and that parking had been turned into a cash cow integral to their business models.

He said: “The more they earn from car parks the more they will dividend it out to their shareholders and then complain that they can’t make a reasonable return on the risks they were taking investing in runways in London.

“But there’s no risk at all, it’s a complete freebie, which is why pension funds now own most of these airports.”

O’Leary said the cost of runways, car parks and terminals was ultimately the result of a “broken” regulatory model that “rewards inefficiency and incentivises airports to squander gazillions”.

A study from Oxford Global Projects commissioned by Heathrow Reimagined, a lobby group formed by airlines including BA, has suggested that Heathrow’s construction costs across a variety of infrastructure including car parks are consistently among the highest in the world.

The report said the cost of the runway alone – covering cement, asphalt and lights – would come in at more than £800,000 per metre, making it one of the most expensive ever built.

Jonathan Simcock, author of The Delivery Gap, an examination of failed infrastructure projects, said companies like Heathrow have an incentive to inflate estimated costs by “including all the bells and whistles they can”.

In so doing, he said, they aim to secure regulatory approval for levels of spending – in Heathrow’s case to be funded by airlines and their customers – that will cover all eventualities and “ensure they are not left holding the baby”.

“If the estimate is inflated then the company ends up making an inappropriate profit. But if it’s undercooked the company gets into trouble, somebody has to bail them out and the country doesn’t get the infrastructure it needs,” he says.

Woldbye has said that the costings for the car parks and the wider runway project could change and that Heathrow is open to conversations about modifying its plans.

However, Simcock warned: “When the conversation is about how it can possibly cost that much, very often it turns out that it should have been, can you really do it for that?

“For very large and highly complex HS2-type things – and Heathrow is big and complex – it is rare to get nice surprises and much more common to get nasty ones.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Making bombs won’t save Volkswagen
data.description:
It summons up dark memories. It may prove ethically troubling, both for its workforce and for its local community.

As reports emerge that Volkswagen may start making parts for the Israeli Iron Dome, lots of difficult questions are being asked about the pivot from making cars to manufacturing military equipment.

However, there is a more serious issue, both for VW and for the rest of Europe’s struggling auto industry.

What makes them think they will be any good at it?

Defence is a high-tech, fiercely competitive industry and not one that anyone can easily conquer.

VW certainly needs to try something. What was once the biggest auto company in the world and the emblem of Germany’s post-war economic miracle is in deep trouble.

Soaring energy costs in Germany have been a huge burden alongside the European Union’s blundering determination to impose a ban on new petrol and diesel cars in the 2030s.

Over the last five years, VW’s share price has fallen by more than 60pc and it has started to cut its workforce. Its net profits fell by 44pc last year. The company had planned to become a major manufacturer of electric vehicles but the new Chinese players appear to have that market sewn up with cheaper – and often better – models. The outlook is grim.

Its solution appears to be a pivot to the defence industry.

While VW has publicly ruled out making weapons, reports emerged this week that its Osnabrück plant, currently threatened with closure, may start making parts for the Iron Dome anti-missile system produced by Israel’s Rafael Advanced Defence Systems.

There were reports last year that Rheinmetall, Germany’s largest defence company, could take over the plant instead. We will see in the next few weeks what happens.

On one level, switching from cars to tanks and guns might make a lot of sense. Defence is booming, especially in Germany where the government is spending tens of billions of euros on re-equipping its armed forces. With the war in Iran, demand is only going to grow in the next few years.

Every government in the world will be looking at installing anti-missile shields – apart from the UK, where we will probably spend the money on extra welfare instead – as well as re-stocking arsenals that are already looking badly depleted.

Who knows, VW might set a template for the entire industry with the likes of BMW, Stellantis (the owner of Fiat and Citroën) and Renault all moving into defence manufacturing as well.

Even Britain’s Jaguar Land Rover could opt to go back to its roots – at least for its Land Rover unit – and start making military off-roaders instead of plush SUVs for the school run in the world’s more prosperous suburbs. It could be the saviour of an industry that looks as if it is heading into terminal trouble.

Here’s the problem, however. It will be a lot harder than it looks.

There may be some superficial similarities. An auto plant needs to bash a lot of metal, just as a defence factory does. It has to keep costs down and the lines rolling, and it needs to be able to put together a lot of complex parts very efficiently.

In a modest way, the auto manufacturers might be able to make a success of it, at least as subcontractors of the major established giants.

Even so, let’s be honest here. This is not the Second World War, when every available scrap of industrial capacity had to be converted to armaments manufacturing to keep the front lines supplied and every production site from bicycle factories to food plants suddenly found themselves making bullets and bombs.

Modern defence manufacturing involves a very different skill-set. It is high-precision and high-tech, and it involves vast quantities of research and development, as well as massive investments in a skilled workforce.

Just take a look at the world’s top defence manufacturers.

The top three are all American, led by Lockheed Martin, RTX and Northrop Grumman, with Britain’s BAE Systems coming in at number four and Italy’s Leonardo at number 11 in the global market.

These are all companies with long and deep histories in the industry, with established contracts and with relationships with the military that stretch back for generations. They spend billions of dollars developing the best kit that can be made and testing it in the most extreme conditions.

The idea that VW or any of the other traditional auto manufacturers can suddenly break into that list is laughable. It is not going to happen. They don’t have the skills or the expertise, nor are the production lines set up in the right way.

Even if they were, it is a fiercely competitive industry, with their customers largely national governments who are going to spend their budgets on domestic manufacturers instead of in other countries.

If there is space for new players, they are far more likely to be from Ukraine – where local manufacturers have been forced to become world leaders in cheap, effective drones – or from Israel – where they have been forced to become experts in missile protection – than from Germany, France or indeed Britain. It isn’t going to happen.

Europe’s traditional auto giants certainly need to do something. With each month that passes, it becomes increasingly clear that attempts to replace petrol cars with battery-powered ones has been catastrophically mismanaged.

Bureaucrats and politicians appear to care more about grandstanding to the green lobby than preserving vital jobs and manufacturing capacity. It is hard to see how any of them are going to survive the Chinese onslaught on the industry.

Even so, these companies are kidding themselves if they think that switching to making bullets and bombs will save them.

It won’t. All it will do is guarantee terminal decline.

data.title: Jim Ratcliffe’s chemical plant suffers £515m loss
data.description:
Sir Jim Ratcliffe’s chemicals empire racked up €593m (£515m) of losses last year, as it warned of further pain from the war in Iran.

Ineos blamed soaring energy costs and Donald Trump’s tariffs as losses jumped from €71.1m the year prior, highlighting mounting economic strain on the billionaire’s sprawling business operations.

As well as a lack of profit, Ineos’s debt pile also rose from €10.6bn to €11.7bn at the end of December 2025.

It comes after S&P Global and Fitch, two of the world’s largest rating agencies, downgraded chunks of Ineos debt in recent months.

The latest results predate the outbreak of war in Iran, with bosses warning that the conflict risks piling more pressure on the debt-laden business.

An Ineos spokesman said: “While we do not have operations in the Middle East and revenue generated in this region is not material to the [Ineos] Group, the length, impact and outcome of the ongoing military conflict in the Middle East is highly unpredictable and there can be no assurances that further unforeseen events related to this conflict will not have a further material adverse affect on our operations in the future.”

The latest results emerged as Ineos warned investors of possible delays to its €4bn (£3.5bn) flagship petrochemicals plant because of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Ineos Group told investors it was facing potential problems completing its Project One plant in Antwerp, Belgium, after two of its components were stranded in the Gulf.

The plant, which will make materials for plastic products and was set to be finished by the end of this year, was already suffering from delays after its environmental permit was suspended in 2023 by the Belgian government.

Ineos has described the €4bn Project One development as the largest investment in the European chemical sector in a generation. It said last June that the plant was 70pc completed.

Project One is made up of heavy modules which have been built across Thailand, the United Arab Emirates and the Philippines before being shipped to Antwerp.

Two of those shipments are stuck in a port in the Gulf.

Iran has effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz since Israel and the US first launched strikes on the country in late February.

Ineos said that rerouting tankers around the Cape of Good Hope, away from escalating tensions in the Middle East, had already cost the business more than €30m over the last two years.

S&P revealed its latest downgrade for Ineos’s debt last month.

It warned that Ineos was facing turmoil from “challenging trading conditions” around the world for the petrochemical sector. At one point, the company was reportedly considering asset sales as it looked to refinance some of its debt pile.

Sir Jim has blamed mounting losses at his Grangemouth chemical works on government carbon taxes and Britain’s sky-high energy costs.

However, he has also triggered controversy over his comments surrounding immigration.

The Manchester United co-owner, one of Britain’s richest men with a £17bn fortune, said earlier this year that Britain was being “colonised by immigrants”. He later apologised for “offending some people” with his language.

Ineos declined to comment.
